note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-10-04-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft): Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) predicts 182.539s but the ffprobed master is 182.204s (0.335s off, past the ±0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 9 blocks concatenated with a uniform ~0.40s gap after each; per-file ffprobe durations carry ~0.037s of mp3 encoder-delay padding each that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates (9 x 0.037 ≈ 0.33s), which is the whole discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset (0.08s mp3-frame grid); block end = next block onset; final end = ffprobed master duration 182.204s.

phrases.text: Start on Support Tickets and look at the left rail.
The SUPPORT group keeps every support surface in one place,
tickets, knowledge base, alerts, training requests.
Today's stop is System Alerts,
the one feed that pulls infrastructure trouble from every site onto a single page.
Click System Alerts.
One rule before anything else.
Reach this page through the sidebar, never by typing its address.
The direct U-R-L still answers four oh four, while this link renders everything.
The page settles inside the shell, breadcrumb up top,
and four K-P-I tiles waiting across the band.
Read the band left to right.
Three alerts open right now.
Fourteen logged today.
And of the open three, one critical against two warnings.
That single critical is the number that decides whether your coffee waits.
Everything below this band exists to explain these four tiles.
Upper left, Alert Volume.
A year of history on one line, January through December,
one hundred forty eight alerts in all and trending down eight percent.
That slope is the long view.
It is the sentence a quarterly health review wants to write.
Next panel, By Severity.
Four levels.
Info carries fifty eight percent of the traffic, warnings twenty eight,
errors ten, critical just four.
Most of what this feed says is routine noise.
Which is exactly why a critical row deserves your full attention when one shows up.
Then System Health.
Eighty six percent healthy, ten warning, four critical.
Hold on that four.
It matches the critical slice next door, one fact drawn two ways.
When two panels agree like that, you can trust the read.
Scroll to the Alert List.
Six columns carry each alert's story:
when it fired, what kind, how severe, which site, what it said, and whether it is still open.
Two buttons above the table stay untouched.
New Record and Export act on the shared tenant, and a support read never creates an alert.
The pager below counts four rows.
Now the rows, top to bottom.
The critical one is a tunnel alert at M-L-A-S,
conveyor belt tension past its threshold, still active.
Behind it, two warnings:
receipt paper running low on a P-O-S at S-S-N-B,
and a chemical tank under twenty percent at Piscataway.
The last row already resolved, a network failover note.
Three rows still active.
That is the Active Alerts tile, itemized.
That is the whole read.
The band for the instant answer.
The panels for trend and mix.
The list for specifics, which type, at which site, still burning or already out.
When a ticket asks whether an outage is one site or the whole platform,
this page and the Command Center's priority board answer it
before the caller finishes explaining.
